crime: The girls knew her for it: a scraped knee pinched to a scratch, a burn pressed small between her fingers.
That afternoon on the stairs, her friend told her she wanted to spend less time together. "It's nothing," she said.
That evening: film night in the recreation room. The two of them sat at the back as always, a seat apart from the rest. The projector was loud.
In the dark she slid a filed edge beneath her friend's ribs and pinched the wound to a pin's width. Her friend stiffened once against the seat and went still. The edge went back up her sleeve.
When the lights came up the girls filed out past what they took for a sleeper. One girl came back to shake her and found her cold — no wound anywhere. A heart given out, they said.

They opened the jacket. The shirt was soaked stiff over the ribs: a bloodstain a hand wide, and under it a puncture the size of a pin. A pin's wound does not bleed that wide. The wound had been pinched small. The blood it had already spilled had not.
Every girl knew whose hands made hurts small. The seat beside the dead girl had been hers — the two of them at the back, apart, as always.
fields.epitaph: She called it nothing every time, and enough nothings were the whole of it.
fields.epithet: The Girl Who Called It Nothing
execution: She was bound upright in a ring-frame set with pins, none yet touching.
A clock drove the ring. At each minute's stroke one pin pressed a finger's depth into her and withdrew.
At each stroke she shrank the wound to nothing. The finger-deep harm the stroke had dealt was already done.
No single puncture was mortal. The clock advanced the ring, and the count went on.
When the count was complete the sum of them killed her.

magic.name: Pinchsmall
magic.text: She can shrink any hurt — wound, ache, or break — until it is nearly nothing. What the hurt has already done remains done.
